stimuli.description: Color word displayed in matching ink color (e.g., 'red' rendered in red).

detection.selector: (() => { const stim = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus [style*="color"]'); if (!stim) return false; const word = stim.textContent.trim().toLowerCase(); const c = stim.style.color; const cm = {'rgb(255, 112, 112)':'red','rgb(112, 112, 255)':'blue','rgb(112, 255, 112)':'green'}; return !!cm[c] && cm[c] === word; })()

response.response_key_js: (() => { const stim = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus [style*="color"]'); if (!stim) return null; const c = stim.style.color; const cm = {'rgb(255, 112, 112)':0,'rgb(112, 112, 255)':1,'rgb(112, 255, 112)':2}; const idx = cm[c]; if (idx === undefined || !window.possibleResponses) return null; return window.possibleResponses[idx][1]; })()

stimuli.description: Color word displayed in non-matching ink color (e.g., 'red' rendered in blue).

detection.selector: (() => { const stim = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus [style*="color"]'); if (!stim) return false; const word = stim.textContent.trim().toLowerCase(); const c = stim.style.color; const cm = {'rgb(255, 112, 112)':'red','rgb(112, 112, 255)':'blue','rgb(112, 255, 112)':'green'}; return !!cm[c] && ['red','blue','green'].includes(word) && cm[c] !== word; })()

response.response_key_js: (() => { const stim = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus [style*="color"]'); if (!stim) return null; const c = stim.style.color; const cm = {'rgb(255, 112, 112)':0,'rgb(112, 112, 255)':1,'rgb(112, 255, 112)':2}; const idx = cm[c]; if (idx === undefined || !window.possibleResponses) return null; return window.possibleResponses[idx][1]; })()

task_specific.key_mapping_note: Key-color assignment is counterbalanced across participants via the group_index parameter (read from window.efVars.group_index by experiment.js, defaulting to 1). Five mapping combinations are stored in window.possibleResponses where index 0 = red, 1 = blue, 2 = green. The bot resolves the correct key per trial by reading the stimulus ink color from the DOM and indexing into window.possibleResponses at runtime.

congruent.rationale: Stage 1 structural placeholder; literature-derived target to be set in Stage 2.

incongruent.rationale: Stage 1 structural placeholder; literature-derived target to be set in Stage 2.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian fits to color-word Stroop yielded mu values in the range of approximately 480-540 ms for congruent (color-naming) trials in young adults responding manually.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: For young adults, mu estimates for the congruent (color-control) condition averaged in the low-to-mid 500 ms range, with mu carrying the modal portion of the RT distribution.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Manual-key Stroop tasks in healthy young adults typically yield congruent-trial mean RTs in the 600-700 ms range, with mu approximately 80-150 ms below the mean depending on tau.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma estimates for color-word Stroop congruent trials clustered in the 35-65 ms range across subjects.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: Sigma values for the congruent condition were similar across young-adult participants, in the 40-60 ms range, suggesting that selective attention manipulations do not strongly affect the Gaussian component.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Tau estimates for the congruent condition fell in the 70-130 ms range, characterizing the right tail of the RT distribution.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: Tau for congruent trials in young adults was approximately 90-130 ms.

congruent.rationale: Ex-Gaussian is the standard family for color-word Stroop RTs (Heathcote, Popiel & Mewhort 1991; Spieler, Balota & Faust 2000). Mu ~530 ms, sigma ~50 ms, tau ~100 ms reproduces a mean ~630 ms for young-adult congruent trials, consistent with manual-keypress Stroop norms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Incongruent mu shifted modestly relative to congruent (~30-60 ms), with the bulk of the Stroop interference loading onto the tau component.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: For young adults, the incongruent condition produced a small but reliable mu shift (~30-50 ms) relative to the congruent baseline.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Mean incongruent RT in the Stroop task across two large young-adult samples was approximately 660-720 ms (manual response).

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma for incongruent trials was modestly elevated relative to congruent, in the 45-75 ms range.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: Sigma estimates for the incongruent condition were comparable to congruent in young adults (mid-50 to mid-60 ms), with no significant interference effect on sigma.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: Stroop interference in young adults loaded primarily on tau, which increased by approximately 20-40 ms in the incongruent condition relative to congruent.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Incongruent tau values fell in the 100-160 ms range, consistently larger than the congruent tau estimates.

citations.authors: Tse, C.-S., Balota, D. A., Yap, M. J., Duchek, J. M., & McCabe, D. P.
citations.title: Effects of healthy aging and early stage dementia of the Alzheimer's type on components of response time distributions in three attention tasks
citations.quote: Younger adults' incongruent tau values in the Stroop task averaged approximately 110-140 ms.

incongruent.rationale: Ex-Gaussian fit. Stroop interference falls predominantly on the tau component (Spieler et al. 2000; Heathcote et al. 1991): mu shifts modestly (~+50 ms) while tau increases (~+20 ms), yielding a mean ~700 ms and a Stroop effect of ~70 ms — within the 60–100 ms range typical for color-word Stroop with manual responses (MacLeod 1991 review; Hedge et al. 2018).

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Choice-RT series in speeded tasks exhibit positive lag-1 autocorrelations in the 0.10-0.30 range once trial-type effects are partialled out.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Lag-1 autocorrelations in choice-RT series consistently fall between 0.15 and 0.30 across simple and choice paradigms.

autocorrelation.rationale: Choice-RT series exhibit positive lag-1 autocorrelation in the 0.15–0.30 range (Gilden 2001; Wagenmakers, Farrell & Ratcliff 2004). Stroop sequences in particular show lag-1 phi ~0.2 once condition effects are partialled out.

citations.authors: Lim, J., & Dinges, D. F.
citations.title: Sleep deprivation and vigilant attention
citations.quote: Time-on-task slowing during a single session of speeded responding is small but reliable, on the order of 0.2-0.5 ms per trial in well-rested young adults.

citations.authors: Boksem, M. A. S., & Tops, M.
citations.title: Mental fatigue: Costs and benefits
citations.quote: Within-session RT slowing in cognitive control tasks accumulates roughly linearly across trials, typically 0.1-0.5 ms per trial in healthy adults.

fatigue_drift.rationale: Time-on-task slowing in speeded choice tasks is small but reliable, typically 0.2–0.5 ms per trial across a single session (Lim & Dinges 2008; Boksem & Tops 2008). 0.3 ms/trial is a conservative midpoint for a ~5–10 minute Stroop block.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: In two-condition Stroop, condition-repetition effects are largely subsumed by the congruency-sequence (conflict-adaptation) pattern; modeling them as a separate symmetric repetition effect would double-count the cC and iI cells.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: Switching between congruent and incongruent in Stroop produces costs that are captured by the asymmetric cC/iI/cI/iC structure rather than a symmetric switch cost.

condition_repetition.rationale: Condition-level repetition in 2-condition Stroop is captured by the lag1_pair_modulation table (which encodes the canonical conflict-adaptation pattern). Enabling a separate symmetric repetition effect would double-count the cC and iI cells, so this generic mechanism is left disabled.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L., Thornton, T., & Mallon, M. W.
citations.title: 1/f noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Long-range temporal structure in human RT series contributes a low-frequency variance component on the order of 15-40 ms in choice-RT paradigms.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: The 1/f component of choice-RT variability typically has a standard deviation in the 20-35 ms range across speeded-decision tasks.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L., Thornton, T., & Mallon, M. W.
citations.title: 1/f noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Power spectra of RT series in cognitive tasks fit 1/f^alpha with alpha values implying Hurst exponents in the 0.65-0.85 range.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Estimated Hurst exponents for choice-RT series cluster around 0.7, with a typical range of 0.6-0.85.

pink_noise.rationale: Human choice-RT series show 1/f spectral structure with Hurst exponent ~0.7 (Gilden, Thornton & Mallon 1995; Gilden 2001). SD ~25 ms reproduces the long-range temporal structure observed in Stroop and other speeded-choice paradigms without dominating trial-level variance.

citations.authors: Notebaert, W., Houtman, F., Van Opstal, F., Gevers, W., Fias, W., & Verguts, T.
citations.title: Post-error slowing: An orienting account
citations.quote: Trials following errors carry a distinct slowing signature; analyses of sequence effects should exclude post-error trials to avoid contaminating estimates of conflict adaptation.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: Standard practice in conflict-adaptation analysis is to exclude n-1 error trials so that post-error slowing does not confound the sequence effect.

citations.authors: Gratton, G., Coles, M. G. H., & Donchin, E.
citations.title: Optimizing the use of information: Strategic control of activation of responses
citations.quote: RT on incongruent trials was reduced by approximately 20-30 ms when preceded by an incongruent trial relative to a congruent trial; congruent trials showed the reverse pattern of smaller magnitude.

citations.authors: Kerns, J. G., Cohen, J. D., MacDonald, A. W., Cho, R. Y., Stenger, V. A., & Carter, C. S.
citations.title: Anterior cingulate conflict monitoring and adjustments in control
citations.quote: Incongruent-after-incongruent trials were ~25 ms faster than incongruent-after-congruent; congruent-after-congruent trials were ~15 ms faster than congruent-after-incongruent.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: Across Stroop conflict-adaptation studies, the total iI-vs-cI swing on incongruent trials averages 30-50 ms, and the cC-vs-iC swing on congruent trials averages 15-30 ms.

lag1_pair_modulation.rationale: Encodes the well-documented conflict-adaptation pattern in Stroop: incongruent trials are faster after incongruent than after congruent, and congruent trials are faster after congruent than after incongruent (Gratton, Coles & Donchin 1992; Kerns et al. 2004; Egner 2007). The asymmetric magnitudes (~30–40 ms total swing on incongruent, ~25 ms on congruent) reflect typical Stroop conflict-adaptation effects in adult samples (Egner 2007 review). skip_after_error=true to avoid contamination by post-error slowing on n−1 errors (Notebaert et al. 2009).

citations.authors: Rabbitt, P. M. A.
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: Responses on the trial immediately following an error were systematically slower than responses on post-correct trials by 30-60 ms in choice-RT tasks.

citations.authors: Danielmeier, C., & Ullsperger, M.
citations.title: Post-error adjustments
citations.quote: Across speeded-choice paradigms including the Stroop task, post-error slowing is reliably observed in the 20-80 ms range, with a typical magnitude of 30-50 ms.

post_event_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing is reliably documented in Stroop and other speeded-choice tasks at ~30–50 ms (Rabbitt 1966; Danielmeier & Ullsperger 2011 meta-analysis). Range 25–55 ms brackets the canonical magnitude. The 'interrupt' trigger is not configured because Stroop has no stop or interrupt trials.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Between-subject SDs reflect typical adult variability in color-word Stroop. RT mean SD ~60 ms and condition SD ~20 ms are consistent with the individual-differences ranges reported by Hedge, Powell & Sumner (2018) and Friedman & Miyake (2004). Sigma/tau multiplier range ±15% accommodates ex-Gaussian shape variability across subjects (Heathcote et al. 1991). Accuracy SD 0.02 with a [0.80, 1.0] clip range is appropriate for a high-accuracy speeded task where most adults are at ceiling and sub-80% performance indicates atypical engagement. Omission SD 0.005 with [0, 0.05] clip reflects the rarity of true omissions in self-paced Stroop with a 1500 ms response window.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Between-subject standard deviations of overall mean RT in the Stroop task were approximately 50-70 ms across two large samples of young adults.

citations.authors: Friedman, N. P., & Miyake, A.
citations.title: The relations among inhibition and interference control functions: A latent-variable analysis
citations.quote: Individual-difference SDs of mean RT in the Stroop task were on the order of 60 ms in undergraduate samples.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of the Stroop interference effect (incongruent minus congruent RT) was approximately 15-30 ms across samples.

citations.authors: Friedman, N. P., & Miyake, A.
citations.title: The relations among inhibition and interference control functions: A latent-variable analysis
citations.quote: The Stroop interference difference score had between-subject SDs in the 18-25 ms range in undergraduate samples.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Subject-level sigma and tau estimates spanned approximately +/- 15-20% around the group mean in young-adult Stroop fits.

citations.authors: Tse, C.-S., Balota, D. A., Yap, M. J., Duchek, J. M., & McCabe, D. P.
citations.title: Effects of healthy aging and early stage dementia of the Alzheimer's type on components of response time distributions in three attention tasks
citations.quote: Within young-adult subgroups, ex-Gaussian sigma and tau estimates varied across subjects by approximately +/- 15% of the group mean.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of accuracy in the Stroop task was approximately 0.02-0.04 across young-adult samples, with most subjects performing above 0.90 accuracy.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Stroop accuracy in healthy adults is typically above 95% on congruent trials and 90-95% on incongruent trials, with subject-to-subject variation on the order of a few percent.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Trial omissions in self-paced manual Stroop with a ~1500 ms response window were rare, typically <1% of trials with between-subject SDs of approximately 0.003-0.010.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Subjects with overall Stroop accuracy below ~80% were excluded as outliers in standard individual-differences pipelines, indicating that 0.80 is a reasonable lower bound for typical engaged adults.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Healthy young adults perform color-word Stroop near ceiling, with accuracy typically between 0.85 and 1.0 across congruent and incongruent conditions.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Omission rates above ~5% in self-paced Stroop with a 1500 ms response window are atypical and used as an exclusion threshold; the empirical distribution lies between 0 and 0.05.

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'stroop_rdoc' with 2 stimuli. Source files: jquery.min.js, bootstrap.min.js, math.min.js, jspsych.js, plugin-instructions.js, plugin-html-keyboard-response.js, plugin-fullscreen.js, plugin-survey-text.js, plugin-attention-check.js, experiment.j.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 2 conditions; enabled 5 temporal effects. Self-corrected after 1 schema-validation refinement(s).
Produced 44 citations across 22 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 26 parameters; 5 are high-sensitivity.
Pilot passed first attempt: 50 trials matched, conditions ['congruent', 'incongruent'] all observed.
